text:
SURVEILLANCE CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND RADIONUCLIDE BONE SCAN

CLINICAL INDICATION: 59-year-old woman with history of left breast triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma (stage IIB at diagnosis, ypT1cN1mi post-neoadjuvant), germline BRCA1 mutation (c.5266dupC). Status post left modified radical mastectomy with contralateral prophylactic mastectomy, post-mastectomy radiation therapy to left chest wall and regional nodes, adjuvant Xeloda (8 cycles completed) and pembro (near completion of planned 1-year course). Prior radiation pneumonitis resolved. Presenting for routine surveillance imaging. History of immune-related hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, peripheral neuropathy, and type 2 diabetes.

TECHNIQUE:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Multidetector CT performed from lung apices through pubic symphysis following administration of 100 mL intravenous iohexol contrast. Acquisitions obtained in portal venous and delayed phases. Multiplanar reformations reviewed. Comparison made to prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 8 months ago, which demonstrated no evidence of disease.

Bone Scan: Planar whole-body radionuclide scintigraphy performed 3 hours following intravenous injection of approximately 25.0 mCi (925 MBq) Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP). Anterior and posterior whole-body images obtained. Supplemental SPECT/CT imaging of the thorax and thoracolumbar spine performed for equivocal foci. Comparison made to bone scan performed approximately 20 months ago.

---

CT FINDINGS:

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:
The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations are identified. Previously described ground-glass opacities in the left lung base and perihilar region (attributed to radiation pneumonitis on prior study) have fully resolved with no residual fibrotic change. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA:
The mediastinal contour is normal. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes identified. The hila are symmetric without adenopathy. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. No evidence of superior vena cava obstruction.

CHEST WALL / POST-SURGICAL CHANGES:
Bilateral tissue expanders are present in the expected subpectoral position, with moderate fill. The left chest wall demonstrates expected post-mastectomy and post-radiation architectural changes with overlying soft tissue thickening and mild subcutaneous fibrotic stranding, unchanged from prior examination. No discrete soft tissue nodularity or abnormal enhancement within the left chest wall to suggest local recurrence. No internal mammary lymph node enlargement. Left axillary nodal basin demonstrates residual post-treatment fibrotic changes; no discrete lymph nodes meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement. Right axillary soft tissues are unremarkable.

ABDOMEN - LIVER:
Two new hepatic lesions are identified that were not present on the prior CT examination from approximately 8 months ago:

1. Segment VI (right hepatic lobe, posterior inferior): A well-circumscribed, predominantly hypoattenuating lesion measuring 1.4 x 1.2 cm in axial dimension (series 4, image 87). On portal venous phase imaging the lesion demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with a hypoattenuating center. On delayed phase imaging there is no fill-in of the central hypodensity. These enhancement characteristics are atypical for a simple cyst or hemangioma and are concerning for a hypovascular neoplastic deposit.

2. Segment VII (right hepatic lobe, posterior superior): A smaller hypoattenuating lesion measuring approximately 0.8 cm in greatest dimension (series 4, image 74), with similar rim-enhancing appearance on post-contrast imaging. The lesion is too small for confident characterization but its morphology and concurrent appearance with the segment VI lesion raise similar concern.

The remainder of the hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous without additional discrete nodularity. The background liver demonstrates mild hepatic steatosis (average Hounsfield units approximately 42 on pre-contrast, compared to approximately 56 in the spleen), consistent with the patient's known metabolic history. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The main portal vein and hepatic veins are patent.

ABDOMEN - OTHER UPPER ABDOMINAL STRUCTURES:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation without focal lesions. The pancreas is normal in caliber and attenuation throughout without ductal dilatation or peripancreatic infiltration. The gallbladder is surgically absent (prior cholecystectomy changes noted; this appears to be a remote incidental finding). The common bile duct measures 5 mm, within normal limits. Both adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable without nodularity. The kidneys are symmetric in size. There are bilateral renal cortical cysts, the largest measuring 1.3 cm in the right kidney upper pole, stable in appearance and consistent with a benign simple cyst (Bosniak I). No hydronephrosis. The stomach and visualized bowel loops are unremarkable without wall thickening or masses.

ABDOMEN/PELVIS - LYMPH NODES:
No enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, iliac, or inguinal lymph nodes meeting size criteria for pathologic involvement. No new para-aortic or celiac axis nodal disease.

PELVIS:
No pelvic lymphadenopathy. The uterus and ovaries are not visualized (consistent with known postmenopausal atrophy; ovaries are not definitively identified, which is within expected limits for a postmenopausal woman). Urinary bladder is partially distended and unremarkable. No pelvic free fluid.

BONES (as visualized on CT):
In the right posterior 8th rib, there is a new focal area of cortical thinning and subtle intramedullary lucency measuring approximately 1.5 cm in longest axis (series 3, image 62), not identified on the prior study. The lesion has an ill-defined zone of transition with no sclerotic rim, and there is no associated soft tissue mass on CT. The vertebral column and visualized appendicular skeleton are otherwise unremarkable without lytic or blastic lesions. No pathologic-appearing compression deformity. Degenerative changes are seen at multiple lumbar levels, most pronounced at L4-L5 and L5-S1, with facet arthrosis and mild bilateral foraminal narrowing, unchanged in severity from prior imaging.

---

BONE SCAN FINDINGS:

Normal distribution of radiotracer is seen throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton with appropriate renal and bladder excretion. Symmetric activity is noted in the calvarium, facial bones, and mandible.

Focal area of moderately increased Tc-99m MDP uptake is identified in the right posterior lateral 8th rib, corresponding to the area of cortical thinning and intramedullary lucency identified on the concurrent CT. The intensity of uptake is moderately elevated and morphologically focal (not elongated in pattern, which would favor a rib fracture). On supplemental SPECT/CT correlative imaging, this focus aligns precisely with the lytic osseous change described on CT, supporting an active metabolic process. The appearance is not typical of a benign traumatic or stress-related rib lesion.

Mild, diffuse, bilaterally symmetric increased tracer uptake is present in both knee joints, stable compared to the prior bone scan and consistent with known bilateral osteoarthritis.

Mild increased uptake is seen at multiple lumbar facet joints, consistent with known degenerative disease, unchanged.

No additional abnormal foci of increased tracer uptake identified in the calvarium, axial skeleton, or extremities beyond those described.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. TWO NEW HEPATIC LESIONS, RIGHT LOBE (segment VI, 1.4 cm; segment VII, 0.8 cm): Both are hypoattenuating with peripheral rim enhancement and absent delayed fill-in, morphology highly concerning for hypovascular hepatic metastases in the setting of this patient's history of triple-negative breast carcinoma. These lesions are new compared to the prior surveillance CT from approximately 8 months ago. Primary hepatocellular lesions (hemangioma, focal nodular hyperplasia) are considered less likely given the enhancement pattern. Tissue sampling is strongly warranted for histologic confirmation.

2. NEW LYTIC LESION, RIGHT POSTERIOR 8TH RIB (1.5 cm): Focal lytic intramedullary lesion with corresponding moderate focal tracer uptake on bone scan. In the clinical context of known primary breast malignancy with residual disease history and new hepatic findings, this appearance is suspicious for a metastatic osseous deposit. Benign etiologies such as traumatic fracture or fibrous dysplasia are considered less likely given the lytic morphology and focal scintigraphic uptake pattern. No cortical breakthrough or soft tissue extension identified on CT.

3. NO EVIDENCE OF PULMONARY METASTASES OR MEDIASTINAL/AXILLARY LYMPHADENOPATHY.

4. POST-SURGICAL AND POST-RADIATION CHANGES IN THE LEFT CHEST WALL: Stable, without imaging findings to suggest locoregional recurrence.

5. RESOLVED RADIATION PNEUMONITIS: Prior ground-glass opacities in the left lung have fully cleared.

6. Bilateral degenerative joint changes at knees and lumbar spine: Stable, consistent with prior imaging and known history.

In summary, the new hepatic lesions and right rib lytic lesion occurring in the context of this patient's history of triple-negative breast carcinoma represent findings that are highly suspicious for distant metastatic recurrence. Urgent clinical correlation and tissue confirmation (particularly of the hepatic lesion, most accessible) are recommended to guide further management.

Attendings: [Radiologist signature block]
